stories.headline: Apple Posts Record $111 Billion Q2 Revenue, Driven by iPhone and Services Surge
stories.summary: Apple reported its strongest March quarter ever with $111.2 billion in revenue, marking a 17% year-over-year increase. iPhone sales jumped 22% to $57 billion while Services revenue hit an all-time high of $31 billion, demonstrating robust performance across all product categories and geographies.
stories.original_title: Apple's Q2 2026 report card: $111 billion in revenue, a record on every line, and a memory bill coming due
stories.why_it_matters: Apple is making more money than ever, which shows people still really want their phones and apps. This success means the company has more cash to invest in new tech or buy other businesses, keeping its power in the market strong.
stories.other_side: Some worry that relying so heavily on one product line makes the company vulnerable if phone sales drop. Also, high profits might lead to less money being spent on improving worker conditions or lowering prices for customers.

stories.headline: Meta Terminates Contract With Workers Who Exposed Ray-Ban Smart Glasses Footage Review
stories.summary: Meta has cancelled its contract with Sama, a Nairobi-based outsourcing firm, after workers revealed they were reviewing footage captured by users of Meta’s Ray-Ban smart glasses. The disclosure highlighted significant privacy concerns regarding the data handling practices associated with Meta's wearable technology.
stories.original_title: Meta cancelled the contract with the people who saw what its glasses see
stories.why_it_matters: This story highlights a major privacy risk with smart glasses that can record video without people knowing. It forces companies to be more careful about how they handle user data to avoid losing public trust.
stories.other_side: Critics argue that strict rules on data collection could slow down innovation and make these useful gadgets too expensive. Others say users should just accept some privacy loss as the price for having cool new technology.

stories.headline: Snap CEO Claims AI Wrote Two-Thirds of Company Code Amid Societal Pushback Warnings
stories.summary: The CEO of Snap stated that artificial intelligence now generates approximately two-thirds of the company's code, signaling a massive shift in software development workflows. However, he cautioned that tech executives are underestimating the growing societal resistance and negative public perception toward rapid AI adoption.
stories.original_title: Snap CEO praises AI for writing two-thirds of the company’s code but warns fellow tech executives underestimate ‘societal pushback’ to the tech
stories.why_it_matters: If AI writes most of the code, software gets built much faster and cheaper. However, this shift changes what jobs programmers will have, moving them from writing code to checking AI work.
stories.other_side: People are worried that relying on AI for code could introduce hidden errors or security holes. There is also fear that this trend will reduce the need for human developers, leading to job losses in the tech sector.

stories.headline: AWS CEO Dismisses AI Job Loss Fears, Announces Plan to Hire 11,000 Interns
stories.summary: Amazon Web Services CEO rejected concerns that AI will eliminate software jobs, asserting that demand for coding talent is accelerating. To support this growth, Amazon plans to hire 11,000 interns in 2026, contrasting with other industry leaders who warn of disruption in the coding sector.
stories.original_title: AWS CEO dismisses AI job loss fears, says Amazon plans to hire 11,000 interns in 2026
stories.why_it_matters: Amazon believes AI creates more jobs than it destroys by increasing demand for tech workers. Hiring thousands of interns suggests they expect a huge growth in projects needing human oversight and creativity.
stories.other_side: Many experts disagree, saying AI tools will soon replace entry-level coding jobs entirely. They warn that training interns for roles that might not exist in five years is a risky bet for young workers.

stories.headline: Reports Confirm OnePlus and Realme Merger Amidst Corporate Uncertainty
stories.summary: Major reports indicate that OnePlus has merged with Realme, another sub-brand of OPPO, consolidating their market positions. Despite these claims, significant details regarding the operational structure and strategic implications of the merger remain unclear and unverified.
stories.original_title: Major reports say OnePlus, Realme merge, but there's a lot still in the dark
stories.why_it_matters: Merging two brands helps companies save money and compete better against bigger rivals like Apple. It simplifies how products are made and sold, potentially leading to better deals for consumers.
stories.other_side: Customers might face fewer choices if two competing brands become one company. There is also uncertainty about whether the merged company will keep the unique features that made each brand special.

stories.headline: Futurist Peter Diamandis Outlines Essential Skills for Children to Thrive in the AI Era
stories.summary: Author and podcaster Peter Diamandis argues that children must cultivate purpose, curiosity, and the right mindset to succeed in an economy dominated by artificial intelligence. His insights focus on adapting educational approaches to prepare the next generation for AI-driven career landscapes.
stories.original_title: Kids need these 3 things to thrive in the AI era, futurist Peter Diamandis says
stories.why_it_matters: As AI takes over routine tasks, humans need to focus on skills machines cannot easily copy, like creativity and empathy. Teaching kids these soft skills now prepares them for future careers that value human connection.
stories.other_side: Some educators argue that focusing too much on "mindset" ignores the need for hard technical skills. They believe students still need strong math and science foundations to understand the AI tools they will use daily.

stories.headline: Big Tech Executive Reveals How Neurodivergence Shaped His Career Success
stories.summary: Wainwright Yu, a Big Tech executive, shares his personal journey managing ADHD and anxiety within the high-pressure technology sector. He discusses how he transformed these neurodivergent traits into professional strengths, offering a perspective on diversity and inclusion in tech leadership.
stories.original_title: I'm a Big Tech executive with ADHD and anxiety. Neurodivergence has its downsides, but I've turned my habits into strengths.
stories.why_it_matters: Sharing stories about neurodivergence helps reduce stigma and encourages diverse hiring in tech. It shows that different ways of thinking can lead to unique solutions and innovations in the workplace.
stories.other_side: Skeptics worry that highlighting individual struggles might distract from systemic issues in corporate culture. Some argue that companies should fix their stressful environments rather than expecting employees to adapt to them.

stories.headline: Cyber Essentials Update Threatens Public Sector Contracts Over Security Compliance
stories.summary: New updates to the Cyber Essentials scheme could jeopardize public sector contracts for organizations failing to meet revised security standards. The changes emphasize the critical need for rigorous cybersecurity preparedness among government suppliers and public service providers.
stories.original_title: Cyber Essentials update could put your public sector contracts at risk
stories.why_it_matters: New security rules force government contractors to protect data better, reducing the risk of hacks. This protects citizens' private information and ensures critical services stay online during cyberattacks.
stories.other_side: Small businesses may struggle to afford the expensive upgrades needed to meet these new standards. This could limit competition if only large companies can afford to comply with the stricter rules.

stories.headline: India Needs China-Style Ecosystem Push to Accelerate Electric Vehicle Transition
stories.summary: A Nomura report asserts that India's EV adoption hinges on rapidly building a robust charging infrastructure and policy framework similar to China's approach. The analysis identifies infrastructure readiness as the primary trigger required to achieve mass market adoption of electric vehicles.
stories.original_title: India's EV transition needs a China-style ecosystem push; scale, policy, charging infra boost: Nomura
stories.why_it_matters: India needs more charging stations to convince people to switch from gas cars to electric ones. Building this infrastructure quickly is key to cutting pollution and meeting climate goals.
stories.other_side: Critics say building charging networks is too costly and slow to keep up with car sales. They argue that focusing on battery technology improvements might be a faster way to boost electric vehicle use.

stories.headline: Groww Shifts Focus from Stocks to Mutual Funds and ETFs Following Regulatory Changes
stories.summary: Groww's Q4 FY26 earnings reveal a strategic shift in customer acquisition as SEBI's regulatory changes caused a sharp decline in F&O trading penetration. Mutual funds and ETFs have emerged as the primary growth drivers for the fintech platform, reshaping its investment funnel.
stories.original_title: Groww Q4 FY26 Earnings Call: Customer acquisition funnel shifts from stocks to mutual funds, ETFs
stories.why_it_matters: Investors are moving away from risky trading toward safer long-term investments like mutual funds. This shift reflects a change in consumer behavior as regulations make quick trades harder and less profitable.
stories.other_side: Some traders feel frustrated by the new rules, saying they limit their freedom to manage money. The platform's pivot might also mean fewer options for those who prefer active, short-term investing strategies.
